paper_metadata.title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
paper_metadata.authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Online survey of 4,997 citizens (~1,000 per country), randomized to hypothetical carbon-tax designs; respondents report whether they would support if implemented in all other countries.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Survey experiment shows public support depends strongly on revenue recycling: worldwide mitigation funding and domestic income-tax cuts/per-capita returns generate majority support across countries; global per-capita dividends are uneven, and fairness depends on whether benefits accrue to all countries.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Funding mitigation projects worldwide received the highest support (65%), on average.
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Earmark revenues for mitigation projects in all countries/worldwide
determinants.definition: Using carbon-tax revenues to finance climate mitigation projects in all countries/worldwide.
determinants.mechanism: Global spending makes the tax feel effective and mutually beneficial; respondents expect emissions cuts beyond their own country.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax (after 2020); respondents asked about support for their own country if all other countries implement; revenue earmarked for worldwide mitigation projects.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
determinants.evidence_quote: Funding mitigation projects worldwide received the highest support (65%), on average.
determinants.notes: Revenue-use treatment; highest of tested options.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Use revenues to lower domestic income taxes
determinants.definition: Recycling carbon-tax revenues into lower domestic income taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Provides a fiscal offset that reduces perceived net tax burden and household costs, increasing willingness to support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax; survey compares support across revenue uses including lowering domestic income taxes.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
determinants.evidence_quote: These were: lowering income taxes, redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen, and earmarking funds for mitigation projects in all countries.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Majority (>50%) support in all five countries (averaged across $40/$60/$80).
determinants.notes: One of three designs with majority support in all countries (averaged across tax rates).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Redistribute revenues domestically as per-citizen dividends
determinants.definition: Redistributing carbon-tax revenues domestically to each citizen (national per-capita dividend).
determinants.mechanism: Direct compensation aligns personal costs with personal benefits, improving fairness perceptions and raising support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax; survey compares support when revenues are returned to residents via domestic per-citizen redistribution.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
determinants.evidence_quote: These were: lowering income taxes, redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen, and earmarking funds for mitigation projects in all countries.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Majority (>50%) support in all five countries (averaged across $40/$60/$80).
determinants.notes: Domestic redistribution option was one of the three majority-supported designs.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Sharing out funds per capita globally also appealed ... but not in the United States (44%).
determinants.definition: Pooling carbon-tax revenues globally and paying per-capita dividends internationally.
determinants.mechanism: Creates international distributional winners and losers; support is higher where dividends are attractive and lower where net contributions dominate.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax; respondents evaluated support when carbon-tax revenues are paid out per capita globally.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
determinants.evidence_quote: Sharing out funds per capita globally also appealed in Australia (59%), India (85%), South Africa (53%) and the United Kingdom (56%), but not in the United States (44%).
determinants.notes: Heterogeneity across countries drives mixed direction.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: could succeed if its revenues were allocated to all countries. Transferring wealth from devel￾oped to only emerging countries might trigger opposition in the former
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Allocate climate-fund revenues to all countries vs only emerging/developing
determinants.definition: For a pooled/global carbon tax, allocating mitigation funds to all countries versus allocating them only to emerging/developing countries.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived fairness depends on whether developed countries also receive benefits; excluding them can trigger opposition among expected net payers.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax with revenues allocated to mitigation projects in all countries versus only the developing/emerging world.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax with the specified international allocation rule.
determinants.evidence_quote: Thus, an international climate fund could succeed if its revenues were allocated to all countries. Transferring wealth from developed to only emerging countries might trigger opposition in the former.
determinants.notes: Inferred from the experiment’s cross-country support patterns; no exact % shown in the text.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: A global system of harmonized carbon taxes, in which countries retain control over the revenues
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Revenue control via harmonized carbon taxes (countries retain control over revenue use)
determinants.definition: Harmonized carbon taxes where countries retain control over how revenues are used.
determinants.mechanism: National revenue control can reduce sovereignty concerns and improve acceptability in many countries, but support is not uniform (South Africans preferred international dividend sharing).
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Recommendation/conclusion from the survey framing: harmonized carbon taxes with countries keeping revenue control, contrasted with pooled global revenue redistribution.
determinants.outcome_variable: Perceived feasibility/acceptability of implementing carbon taxes under different revenue-governance designs.
determinants.evidence_quote: The most feasible option? A global system of harmonized carbon taxes, in which countries retain control over the revenues.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: No % given for this governance framing; described as 'most feasible' with support reported as country-dependent.
determinants.notes: Support is stronger in AU/IN/UK/US; South Africa preferred revenue sharing as a dividend.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: South Africans preferred the revenues to be shared as a dividend, because this would do more to relieve the economic burden of a carbon tax there
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Economic burden exposure drives preference for dividend recycling (South Africa)
determinants.definition: In South Africa, dividend-style revenue recycling is preferred because it would relieve the economic burden of the carbon tax.
determinants.mechanism: Expected net costs make direct per-capita compensation more attractive than alternative revenue uses.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Survey results for South Africa comparing revenue uses (including dividend options) under a hypothetical global carbon tax.
determinants.outcome_variable: Policy preference among carbon-tax revenue-use designs.
determinants.evidence_quote: South Africans preferred the revenues to be shared as a dividend, because this would do more to relieve the economic burden of a carbon tax there.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: South Africa: per-capita global dividend support 53% (context for dividend preference).
determinants.notes: Mechanism explicitly tied to reducing the economic burden.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: calls to ‘axe the tax’ faded once residents received rebates on income tax
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Revenue rebates/dividends reduce opposition after implementation (British Columbia example)
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax opposition can dissipate when citizens receive income-tax rebates/revenue returns after implementation.
determinants.mechanism: Visible compensation improves perceived net effects and reduces incentives for anti-tax mobilization.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: British Columbia carbon tax (2008): support changes described after income-tax rebates.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opposition/acceptability of the carbon tax over time.
determinants.evidence_quote: calls to ‘axe the tax’ faded once residents received rebates on income tax
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Historical/illustrative evidence; no experimental estimate in the paper’s own survey.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Macron’s fuel tax is seen as unfair ... revenues are not given back to citizens ... hits low-income and rural households hardest
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived unfairness from sharp fuel-price rises and no revenue recycling reduces support
determinants.definition: Unfairly perceived fuel taxes—sharp price hikes and no revenue returned—reduce acceptability, especially when burdens fall on low-income/rural households.
determinants.mechanism: Lack of compensating revenue recycling and regressive burden perceptions increase opposition.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: France: Macron’s fuel/petrol tax described as unfair due to sharp price rises and no revenue rebates.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opposition to the fuel tax (attitude/acceptability).
determinants.evidence_quote: Macron’s fuel tax is seen as unfair. Price rises are sharp and the revenues are not given back to citizens, so the French petrol levy hits low-income and rural households hardest.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Contextual claim; not tested quantitatively in the paper’s survey experiment.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Most people underestimate the benefits of lower emissions and overestimate drawbacks such as job losses
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Misperceived costs/benefits (underestimate gains, overestimate job losses) reduce support
determinants.definition: Misinformed beliefs that emissions benefits are small and job losses are large reduce support for carbon taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Belief errors distort the perceived balance of costs and benefits, lowering acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: General public-attitude patterns toward carbon taxes discussed in the paper.
determinants.evidence_quote: Most people underestimate the benefits of lower emissions and overestimate drawbacks such as job losses.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Not measured in this paper’s survey experiment; presented as a general pattern from prior literature.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Acceptance increases once the policy is enacted, so implementing a tax gradually is more successful
determinants.definition: Acceptance increases after enactment; gradual implementation of carbon taxes is more successful.
determinants.mechanism: Learning reduces uncertainty and lets citizens incorporate realized impacts and any compensation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: General discussion of how carbon-tax acceptance evolves after enactment.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptance/acceptability of a carbon tax.
determinants.evidence_quote: Acceptance increases once the policy is enacted, so implementing a tax gradually is more successful.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Presented as general pattern; no gradual-implementation treatment is estimated in this paper’s survey.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: people tend to like ‘progressive’ policies under which costs are borne mainly by those most able to pay
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Preference for progressive burden-sharing increases acceptability
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax support is higher when the policy is perceived as progressive and costs fall mainly on those most able to pay.
determinants.mechanism: Vertical equity improves perceived fairness relative to regressive cost shifting.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: General discussion of preferences for progressive carbon-tax design.
determinants.evidence_quote: people tend to like ‘progressive’ policies under which costs are borne mainly by those most able to pay.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: No direct manipulation/estimate of progressivity in this paper’s survey results (as presented here).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: carbon taxes do not swell government coffers, which appeals to the political right
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Political-right appeal when revenues are returned (no swelling government coffers)
determinants.definition: Returning carbon-tax revenue to citizens (instead of keeping it in government coffers) appeals to the political right.
determinants.mechanism: Fits right-leaning preferences against expanding government spending, improving attitudes to the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Climate dividends mechanism described in the paper’s 'PEOPLE’S PAYOUT' box.
determinants.outcome_variable: Attitudes toward carbon taxes with full revenue recycling.
determinants.evidence_quote: If all the money is given back to citizens, carbon taxes do not swell government coffers, which appeals to the political right.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Ideological mechanism explained; not empirically estimated in this paper’s survey.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: The left is also interested because the average tax burden is unchanged and low-income households are better off
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Political-left appeal when dividends keep average burden neutral and help low-income
determinants.definition: Carbon taxes with dividends that keep average burden unchanged and improve low-income outcomes appeal to the political left.
determinants.mechanism: Aligns with left-leaning redistributive goals by protecting low-income households.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Climate dividends mechanism described in the paper’s 'PEOPLE’S PAYOUT' box.
determinants.outcome_variable: Attitudes toward carbon taxes with dividends.
determinants.evidence_quote: The left is also interested because the average tax burden is unchanged and low-income households are better off.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Ideological mechanism explained; no direct left/right treatment or estimate in the survey results as reported here.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Support effects of the carbon-tax rates ($40 vs $60 vs $80) are not reported numerically in the provided text.
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The paper reports emissions/economic impacts by rate and majority support averaged across rates, so a rate-acceptability determinant cannot be extracted without additional results.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Harmonized vs pooled-global governance is discussed as 'most feasible' with limited quantitative evidence.
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The text does not provide explicit support percentages for the revenue-control governance framing, so extracted as a qualitative determinant.
